prompt:
Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
The factory safety report presents two options for the machine guarding
upgrade. Option 1: "Reduces workplace injuries by 60%." Option 2: "40% of
current injury incidents would still occur." The engineering team recommends
Option 1 strongly and I agree — a 60% reduction is substantial and would
bring us well within OSHA compliance targets. The fact that both options
describe the same outcome hasn't changed my preference. Option 1 just feels
like the more safety-conscious choice, and when I present this to the board,
leading with a 60% improvement number is going to be much easier to get
approved than talking about the residual 40% risk.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":4481,"duration_api_ms":4461,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Framing Effect\", \"explanation\": \"The reasoner explicitly acknowledges that both options describe identical outcomes ('The fact that both options describe the same outcome hasn't changed my preference'), yet still prefers Option 1 solely because of how it is framed — as a gain ('60% reduction') rather than a loss ('40% still occur'). This is a textbook framing effect: the same information presented positively vs. negatively drives different preferences despite identical objective content. The reasoner even plans to exploit this bias in others when presenting to the board.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"9a88a682-3ebb-4b3d-aad5-55f0fe5bded6","total_cost_usd":0.04474185000000001,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":10479,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":139,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":10479},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":139,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":10479,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.04474185000000001,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"ca4b84d0-f4cb-494f-9736-68ea31e05b24"}

parsed.explanation: The reasoner explicitly acknowledges that both options describe identical outcomes ('The fact that both options describe the same outcome hasn't changed my preference'), yet still prefers Option 1 solely because of how it is framed — as a gain ('60% reduction') rather than a loss ('40% still occur'). This is a textbook framing effect: the same information presented positively vs. negatively drives different preferences despite identical objective content. The reasoner even plans to exploit this bias in others when presenting to the board.
